text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

i's day-by-day guide to the Games and how to follow all the biggest events over the next fortnight

Paris 2024officially kicks off with its opening ceremony on 26 July, but Olympic action will be underway from 24 July when men’s football begins.

It is just three years since Tokyo as that Games was suspended a year due to Covid. Plenty has changed in that time, however, and one sport will be making its Olympic debut this summer.

“Breaking” – popularly known as breakdancing – will now be a medal event after its success since being introduced at the 2018 Youth Olympics.

British eyes will be onTeam GBathletes across the board- 327 of them in total – especially after the remarkable medal successes ofLondon 2012, Rio 2016 andTokyo 2020, British athletes coming fourth overall last time out.

Here isi‘s day-by-day guide to what to watch at the Olympics and when the key events are on. All times are in UK BST.

The biggest events will be shown onBBC OneandBBC Two, with additional coverage on iPlayer, the red button and online.

France-watch

The hosts have top billing two days before the Olympics officially start. There will be no Kylian Mbappe, after hisEuro 2024escapades, but there will be Premier League representation in the shape ofCrystal Palace’sJean-Philippe Mateta.

Veteran-watch

Australia’s Matildas squad features no fewer than eight players heading to their third Games, including Arsenal’s Caitlin Foord. Speaking of experience, South Korean handball player Ryu Eun-hee has 89 caps for her country and has scored 323 goals.

River-watch

This will be the first opening ceremony that is not held in the main stadium. Instead teams will be ferried down the Seine in barges.

Medal-watch

Great Britain’s men’s hockey team are tipped for success at these games – their journey starts here. If GB can make it to the 4x100m swimming relay final it will be a welcome bonus.

Scenery-watch

Elancourt Hill is the site for the mountain biking competition. It is a 231-metre tall knoll that was fashioned out of the surplus from sandstone quarries. There are views of the Eiffel Tower – not that the riders will be looking.

Whitlock-watch

This will be the fourth and final Olympics for the decorated GB gymnastMax Whitlock. He has six medals, stretching back to London 2012. What price on a seventh?

Medal-watch

This day could begin and end in glory for Great Britain, withAlex Yeein the men’s triathlon to accompany your breakfast, and the GB rugby sevens team in the evening. And of course there is swimming.

Red-eye watch

If you are wondering why the surfing event is at such an ungodly hour, it is because it is being held in Tahiti. Closer to home, there will be more chlorine-infused medal action in the pool.

Oar-watch

Britain’s rowing record at Tokyo 2020 is probably best left consigned to history. But hopes are higher this time. Eslewhere, the women’s gymnastics will be a must-watch.

Josh-watch

Josh Kerr is a poster-boy for British athletics, ever since his World Championship win last year. His quest for Olympic gold in the 1500m starts here.

Brit-watch

Tom Pidcock will have hopefully recovered from his bout of Covid which forced him out of the Tour de France as he takes on the road race. On the athletics track, Dina Asher-Smith will hopefully be involved in the 100m final.

Shotgun-watch

Amber Rutter is a good shout for a medal in Paris. She has extra motivation after being ruled out of Tokyo 2020 after catching Covid on the eve of the Games. Meanwhile, Noah Lyles is the favourite for the men’s 100m.

8.30am – Shooting Men’s rapid fire final9.55am – Badminton Women’s singles gold match10.45am – Artistic Gymnastics Four finals2.40pm – Badminton Men’s singles gold match4.30pm – Basketball 3×3 Men’s and women’s semi-finals6.58pm – Track cycling Women’s team sprint gold match7.30pm – Athletics Men’s pole vault final, Women’s discus final, Women’s 5,000m final, Women’s 800m final9.05pm – Basketball 3×3 Women’s and Men’s gold matches

Bling-watch

The track cycling is underway and GB’s sprint team will hopefully be in the reckoning for medals. Meanwhile in the athletics, Keely Hodgkinson will be aiming for an improvement on her Tokyo silver medal.

Air-watch

Sky Brown and Lola Tambling carry GB hopes in the skateboard bowl – the former won bronze at Tokyo 2020. Daryll Neita and Dina Asher-Smith may well be in the 200m final as well.

8.30am – Handball Men’s QF10am – Basketball Women’s QF11.55am – Sport climbing Women’s speed final2pm – Weightlifting Men’s 61kg final4.30pm – Skateboarding Men’s park final6.30pm – Artistic Swimming Team routine8pm – Taekwondo finals9.34pm – Boxing Men’s finals

Oldie-watch

For a youth-focused sport like skateboarding, Britain’s Andy Macdonald sticks out like a… well, a 50-year-old at the skatepark. Mainly because he is 50 years old. He is a legend of the sport, creator of at least two tricks.

Bol-watch

Dutch superstar Femke Bol is almost unbeatable in the 400m hurdles, and we can expect similar here. Early in the morning, the Seine will hopefully be clean enough for a 10km swim.

Baton-watch

Will GB make it to the men’s 4x100m final? There is a chance they will.

New sport-watch

Breaking – acrobatic dancing to hip-hop music – makes its first appearance at a Games. Conversely, this is the last Olympics where we will see modern pentathlon in its current guise.

Campbell watch

Weightlifter Emily Campbell(look out for the nails) could make it a successful final day for Team GB.

Watch every moment of the Olympic Games Paris 2024 live only ondiscovery+, the streaming home of the Olympics.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Paris 2024 Olympic TV guide: How to watch and when Team GB stars are in action
author: Sportsbeat
description: i's day-by-day guide to the Games and how to follow all the biggest events over the next fortnight
